name: Runge Kutta group inverse formula
statement_text:
Given α ∈ G1 and t ∈ T , written in the form (V, E), then

                                              
                                                #P
                                −1
                            α        (t) =             (−α(ti )).                (383b)
                                             P ∈P(S) i=1
proof_text:
Proof. Construct a mapping β ∈ G1 equal to the right-hand side of (383b).
We show that for any t ∈ T , (αβ)(t) = 0 so that αβ = 1. Let t = (V, E).
For any partition P with components (Vi , Ei ), for i = 1, 2, . . . , n, we consider
the set of possible combinations of {1, 2, . . . , n}, with the restriction that if
C is such a combination, then no edge (v1 , v2 ) ∈ E exists with v1 ∈ Vi and
v2 ∈ Vj , with i and j distinct members of C. Let C(P ) denote the set of all

such combinations of P ∈ P(t). Given C ∈ P , denote by C the complement
of C in P .
   The value of (αβ)(t) can be written in the form
                     
                                    α(ti )(−1)#C   α(tj ).
                   P ∈P(t) C∈C(P ) i∈C                   j∈C

For any particular partition P , the total contribution is
                            
                                                #P
                                     (−1)n−#C         α(ti ).
                           C∈C(P )              i=1
                       "
This is zero because    C∈C(P ) (−1)
                                       n−#C
                                              = 0.
proof_latex:
\begin{proof}
Construct a mapping $\beta \in G_1$ equal to the right-hand side of \eqref{eq:383b}.
We show that for any $t \in T$, $(\alpha\beta)(t) = 0$ so that $\alpha\beta = 1$. Let $t = (V, E)$.
For any partition $P$ with components $(V_i, E_i)$, for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$, we consider
the set of possible combinations of $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$, with the restriction that if
$C$ is such a combination, then no edge $(v_1, v_2) \in E$ exists with $v_1 \in V_i$ and
$v_2 \in V_j$, with $i$ and $j$ distinct members of $C$. Let $\mathcal{C}(P)$ denote the set of all
such combinations of $P \in \mathcal{P}(t)$. Given $C \in P$, denote by $\overline{C}$ the complement
of $C$ in $P$.

The value of $(\alpha\beta)(t)$ can be written in the form
\[
\sum_{P \in \mathcal{P}(t)} \sum_{C \in \mathcal{C}(P)} \left( \prod_{i \in C} \alpha(t_i) \right) (-1)^{\#C} \left( \prod_{j \in \overline{C}} \alpha(t_j) \right).
\]

For any particular partition $P$, the total contribution is
\[
\sum_{C \in \mathcal{C}(P)} (-1)^{n-\#C} \prod_{i=1}^{\#P} \alpha(t_i).
\]
This is zero because $\sum_{C \in \mathcal{C}(P)} (-1)^{n-\#C} = 0$.
\end{proof}

variables.name: α
variables.definition: α ∈ G1, an element of the group G1 (presumably a mapping on trees)

variables.name: G1
variables.definition: A group (or algebraic structure) related to trees, likely defined earlier in the text

variables.name: t
variables.definition: t ∈ T, a tree in the set T of trees

variables.name: T
variables.definition: The set of trees (rooted trees, as typical in Butcher series context)

variables.name: S
variables.definition: A tree t written in the form (V, E), where V is the set of vertices and E is the set of edges

variables.name: P(S)
variables.definition: The set of all partitions of S; a partition P ∈ P(S) is a forest formed by possibly removing some edges from E, with components being trees (Vi, Ei)

variables.name: #P
variables.definition: The number of components (trees) in the partition P

variables.name: ti
variables.definition: The tree represented by (Vi, Ei), which is a component of the partition P

preamble: The context involves the group G1 of mappings on trees, where α is an element with an inverse α^{-1}. Trees are represented as (V, E), and partitions of a tree S are forests obtained by removing edges, with components ti. The formula gives α^{-1}(t) as a sum over all partitions of S, each term being a product over the components of the partition.
